title: Acute Urinary Retention
oneLiner: Failure to void urine with painful palpable bladder; elderly males most common; risk of urosepsis, AKI, and permanent bladder injury.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Urosepsis / septic shock:** Fever, hypotension, tachycardia, delirium. Single finding: lactate >2 mmol/L or bacteraemia on blood cultures. Must start antibiotics *before* catheterisation (if possible) or immediately after.
- **Acute kidney injury from post-renal obstruction:** Palpable bladder + anuria + rising creatinine ± hyperkalaemia. Single finding: K+ >6.0 mmol/L on VBG. Requires urgent catheterisation and cardiac monitoring.
- **Cauda equina syndrome (spinal cord compression):** Acute retention + saddle anaesthesia + loss of anal tone + bilateral leg weakness. Single finding: absent perianal pinprick sensation. Requires immediate MRI spine – do not catheterise until neurosurgery consulted unless septic.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Inability to pass urine for hours (often >12h)
  - Severe suprapubic pain (constant, colicky)
  - Urgency with dribbling (overflow incontinence)
  - Lower abdominal distension – "cannot sit still"
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable, tender, dull-to-percuss suprapubic mass (bladder)
  - Residual volume >500 mL (by bladder scanner or straight catheter)
  - ± Costovertebral angle tenderness (pyelonephritis)
  - ± Neurological exam: perianal sensation, anal tone, lower limb reflexes (rule out cord lesion)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Bladder outlet obstruction (BPH, urethral stricture):** Palpable bladder, normal neurological exam, elderly male.
  - **Neurogenic bladder (cauda equina, spinal injury, MS):** Palpable bladder + abnormal neurology (saddle anaesthesia, flaccid anal tone).
  - **Medication-induced (anticholinergics, opioids, sympathomimetics):** Gradual onset, no focal neurology, recent drug change.
  - **Anuria from AKI (prerenal/renal):** No palpable bladder, low urine output from intrinsic cause, not retention.
  - **Pelvic mass (faecal impaction, tumour, pregnancy):** Palpable mass not clearly bladder, rectal/vaginal exam findings.
- **Splitter:**
  - **Palpable bladder on exam → retention; no palpable bladder → suspect prerenal AKI or high obstruction (ureteric) – needs renal ultrasound.**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Bladder scanner or straight catheter (residual volume >500 mL = retention – catheterise immediately if not contraindicated)
  - Urine dipstick + urine culture (if febrile or cloudy)
  - VBG: lactate, K+, creatinine (quickly screen for AKI/hyperkalaemia)
  - ECG if K+ >5.5 or hypotension
- **Bloods:**
  - U&E, creatinine, eGFR
  - FBC, CRP
  - Blood cultures (if febrile or septic)
  - PSA (after catheterisation, once stable – not acutely)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Renal ultrasound** (hydronephrosis? residual after catheter?) – do in all with AKI or suspected obstruction; if normal bladder after catheterisation, likely resolved.
  - Second-line: **CT KUB** (if ultrasound shows hydronephrosis but no ureteric stone on USS, or if suspected stone as cause) – avoid contrast if creatinine rising.
- **Special tests:**
  - Post-void residual (if chronic) – not in acute setting: measure after trial without catheter.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Neurological examination (perineal sensation, anal tone, lower limb reflexes) – missing cauda equina = permanent paralysis.**
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Catheterise:** Urethral – 16Fr or 18Fr Foley, use lignocaine gel. If unable (e.g. stricture, known urological), call urology for suprapubic (do not attempt blindly).
  - **Drain bladder completely:** record residual volume. If >1 L, monitor for post-obstructive diuresis.
  - **Urine culture** if cloudy/febrile – start antibiotics per local policy (e.g. nitrofurantoin if not obstructed; ceftriaxone 2 g IV if septic).
  - **Fluids:** normal saline 500 mL bolus only if hypotensive (risk of diuresis-induced overload if too aggressive).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **Monitor urine output hourly** – if >200 mL/h for >2 h, check U&E 4-hourly, replace volume with 0.45% saline or dextrose-saline (per electrolyte results).
  - **Start alpha-blocker** (e.g. tamsulosin 400 mcg OD) for BPH.
  - **Trial without catheter (TWOC)** after 24–48 h if no sepsis, residual <1 L, and improvement.
  - **If cauda equina:** immediate neurosurgery referral + MRI spine (do not delay by catheterising for hours).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Urology follow-up: outpatient flow studies, cystoscopy (if stricture), consider TURP (if BPH with recurrent retention).
  - Intermittent self-catheterisation (if chronic retention with normal voiding between).
  - Indwelling catheter (if permanent obstruction, failed TWOC, or poor surgical candidate).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 1–2 L/day of 0.9% saline or 5% dextrose; adjust if diuresis (see above)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (or 20 mg if eGFR <30 or elderly frail); check weight/creatinine
  - **Diet:** as tolerated, unless NBM for procedure (TWOC)
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly routine; if post-obstructive diuresis → hourly urine output + 4-hourly U&E
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Tamsulosin** 400 micrograms (modified-release capsule) PO once daily – for BPH, start immediately after catheterisation.
  - **Antibiotics (septic):** Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once then co-amoxiclav 625/125 mg TDS PO (if good response) – check local sensitivity.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Alfuzosin 10 mg PO OD (alternative alpha-blocker)
  - Doxazosin 1–4 mg PO OD (titrate from 1 mg; risk of hypotension)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Alpha-blockers: caution in elderly postural hypotension; avoid with tadalafil (synergistic hypotension).
  - Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, tolterodine) – contraindicated in acute retention (may worsen obstruction).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Alpha-blockers: no renal adjustment needed; severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C) – avoid or reduce dose.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving tamsulosin without checking lying/standing BP** – profound hypotension in volume-depleted or frail.
  - **Antibiotic choice:** avoid single-dose gentamicin in elderly with AKI unless guided by levels.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Urosepsis** (if infected urine upstream)
  - **Acute kidney injury** (post-renal, reversible with catheterisation)
  - **Bladder rupture** (rare; suspect if severe pain + peritonism after failed catheter)
  - **Catheter trauma** (haematuria, false passage)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Recurrent UTIs** (catheter-associated)
  - **Bladder decompensation** (detrusor failure, chronic retention)
  - **Hydronephrosis → chronic kidney disease** (if untreated)
  - **Bladder stones** (stasis)
  - **Overflow incontinence** (social burden)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Good** if cause reversible (drugs, constipation, acute BPH) – 40–60% success with TWOC after alpha-blocker.
- **Poor** if neurogenic (cauda equina) or chronic obstruction with CKD – often permanent catheterisation or dialysis.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a bedside bladder scanner or straight catheter before calling senior.** Palpation alone can be misleading (obesity, ascites).
- **In elderly, review medication list for anticholinergics, tricyclics, decongestants, or opioids** – these are common precipitants.
- **If post-obstructive diuresis (>200 mL/h), measure U&E 4-hourly and replace volume with 0.45% saline** – do not give plain 5% dextrose (hyponatraemia risk).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay catheterisation for imaging** if bladder clearly palpable and patient in pain – time is bladder.
- **Do not attempt suprapubic catheterisation** without ultrasound guidance or urology input – high risk of bowel perforation.
- **Do not give fluids aggressively before catheterisation** if hypotensive – post-obstructive diuresis may be triggered; give only if septic shock.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Unable to pass urine after catheter removal (for ≥6 h)
  - Fever, rigors, or haematuria (sepsis/blocked catheter)
  - Sudden loss of leg/ perineal sensation (cauda equina)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urology clinic in 2–6 weeks for TWOC or further workup
  - GP to review medication list and stop offending drugs
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior urology:**
  - Failed urethral catheterisation (after two attempts by trained clinician)
  - Suspected cauda equina (immediate MRI – also call neurosurgery)
  - Post-obstructive diuresis >300 mL/h for >4 h with electrolyte derangement
- **Call ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L with shock
  - GCS <13 or seizure from uraemia/hyperkalaemia
  - K+ >6.5 mmol/L (give calcium gluconate + insulin/dextrose, then catheterise)

rawMarkdown:
## Acute Urinary Retention
**One-liner:** Failure to void urine with painful palpable bladder; elderly males most common; risk of urosepsis, AKI, and permanent bladder injury.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Urosepsis / septic shock:** Fever, hypotension, tachycardia, delirium. Single finding: lactate >2 mmol/L or bacteraemia on blood cultures. Must start antibiotics *before* catheterisation (if possible) or immediately after.
- **Acute kidney injury from post-renal obstruction:** Palpable bladder + anuria + rising creatinine ± hyperkalaemia. Single finding: K+ >6.0 mmol/L on VBG. Requires urgent catheterisation and cardiac monitoring.
- **Cauda equina syndrome (spinal cord compression):** Acute retention + saddle anaesthesia + loss of anal tone + bilateral leg weakness. Single finding: absent perianal pinprick sensation. Requires immediate MRI spine – do not catheterise until neurosurgery consulted unless septic.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Inability to pass urine for hours (often >12h)
  - Severe suprapubic pain (constant, colicky)
  - Urgency with dribbling (overflow incontinence)
  - Lower abdominal distension – "cannot sit still"
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable, tender, dull-to-percuss suprapubic mass (bladder)
  - Residual volume >500 mL (by bladder scanner or straight catheter)
  - ± Costovertebral angle tenderness (pyelonephritis)
  - ± Neurological exam: perianal sensation, anal tone, lower limb reflexes (rule out cord lesion)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Bladder outlet obstruction (BPH, urethral stricture):** Palpable bladder, normal neurological exam, elderly male.
  - **Neurogenic bladder (cauda equina, spinal injury, MS):** Palpable bladder + abnormal neurology (saddle anaesthesia, flaccid anal tone).
  - **Medication-induced (anticholinergics, opioids, sympathomimetics):** Gradual onset, no focal neurology, recent drug change.
  - **Anuria from AKI (prerenal/renal):** No palpable bladder, low urine output from intrinsic cause, not retention.
  - **Pelvic mass (faecal impaction, tumour, pregnancy):** Palpable mass not clearly bladder, rectal/vaginal exam findings.
- **Splitter:**
  - **Palpable bladder on exam → retention; no palpable bladder → suspect prerenal AKI or high obstruction (ureteric) – needs renal ultrasound.**

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Bladder scanner or straight catheter (residual volume >500 mL = retention – catheterise immediately if not contraindicated)
  - Urine dipstick + urine culture (if febrile or cloudy)
  - VBG: lactate, K+, creatinine (quickly screen for AKI/hyperkalaemia)
  - ECG if K+ >5.5 or hypotension
- **Bloods:**
  - U&E, creatinine, eGFR
  - FBC, CRP
  - Blood cultures (if febrile or septic)
  - PSA (after catheterisation, once stable – not acutely)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Renal ultrasound** (hydronephrosis? residual after catheter?) – do in all with AKI or suspected obstruction; if normal bladder after catheterisation, likely resolved.
  - Second-line: **CT KUB** (if ultrasound shows hydronephrosis but no ureteric stone on USS, or if suspected stone as cause) – avoid contrast if creatinine rising.
- **Special tests:**
  - Post-void residual (if chronic) – not in acute setting: measure after trial without catheter.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Neurological examination (perineal sensation, anal tone, lower limb reflexes) – missing cauda equina = permanent paralysis.**

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Catheterise:** Urethral – 16Fr or 18Fr Foley, use lignocaine gel. If unable (e.g. stricture, known urological), call urology for suprapubic (do not attempt blindly).
  - **Drain bladder completely:** record residual volume. If >1 L, monitor for post-obstructive diuresis.
  - **Urine culture** if cloudy/febrile – start antibiotics per local policy (e.g. nitrofurantoin if not obstructed; ceftriaxone 2 g IV if septic).
  - **Fluids:** normal saline 500 mL bolus only if hypotensive (risk of diuresis-induced overload if too aggressive).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **Monitor urine output hourly** – if >200 mL/h for >2 h, check U&E 4-hourly, replace volume with 0.45% saline or dextrose-saline (per electrolyte results).
  - **Start alpha-blocker** (e.g. tamsulosin 400 mcg OD) for BPH.
  - **Trial without catheter (TWOC)** after 24–48 h if no sepsis, residual <1 L, and improvement.
  - **If cauda equina:** immediate neurosurgery referral + MRI spine (do not delay by catheterising for hours).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Urology follow-up: outpatient flow studies, cystoscopy (if stricture), consider TURP (if BPH with recurrent retention).
  - Intermittent self-catheterisation (if chronic retention with normal voiding between).
  - Indwelling catheter (if permanent obstruction, failed TWOC, or poor surgical candidate).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 1–2 L/day of 0.9% saline or 5% dextrose; adjust if diuresis (see above)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (or 20 mg if eGFR <30 or elderly frail); check weight/creatinine
  - **Diet:** as tolerated, unless NBM for procedure (TWOC)
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly routine; if post-obstructive diuresis → hourly urine output + 4-hourly U&E

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Tamsulosin** 400 micrograms (modified-release capsule) PO once daily – for BPH, start immediately after catheterisation.
  - **Antibiotics (septic):** Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once then co-amoxiclav 625/125 mg TDS PO (if good response) – check local sensitivity.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Alfuzosin 10 mg PO OD (alternative alpha-blocker)
  - Doxazosin 1–4 mg PO OD (titrate from 1 mg; risk of hypotension)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Alpha-blockers: caution in elderly postural hypotension; avoid with tadalafil (synergistic hypotension).
  - Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, tolterodine) – contraindicated in acute retention (may worsen obstruction).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Alpha-blockers: no renal adjustment needed; severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C) – avoid or reduce dose.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving tamsulosin without checking lying/standing BP** – profound hypotension in volume-depleted or frail.
  - **Antibiotic choice:** avoid single-dose gentamicin in elderly with AKI unless guided by levels.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Urosepsis** (if infected urine upstream)
  - **Acute kidney injury** (post-renal, reversible with catheterisation)
  - **Bladder rupture** (rare; suspect if severe pain + peritonism after failed catheter)
  - **Catheter trauma** (haematuria, false passage)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Recurrent UTIs** (catheter-associated)
  - **Bladder decompensation** (detrusor failure, chronic retention)
  - **Hydronephrosis → chronic kidney disease** (if untreated)
  - **Bladder stones** (stasis)
  - **Overflow incontinence** (social burden)

### Prognosis
- **Good** if cause reversible (drugs, constipation, acute BPH) – 40–60% success with TWOC after alpha-blocker.
- **Poor** if neurogenic (cauda equina) or chronic obstruction with CKD – often permanent catheterisation or dialysis.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a bedside bladder scanner or straight catheter before calling senior.** Palpation alone can be misleading (obesity, ascites).
- **In elderly, review medication list for anticholinergics, tricyclics, decongestants, or opioids** – these are common precipitants.
- **If post-obstructive diuresis (>200 mL/h), measure U&E 4-hourly and replace volume with 0.45% saline** – do not give plain 5% dextrose (hyponatraemia risk).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay catheterisation for imaging** if bladder clearly palpable and patient in pain – time is bladder.
- **Do not attempt suprapubic catheterisation** without ultrasound guidance or urology input – high risk of bowel perforation.
- **Do not give fluids aggressively before catheterisation** if hypotensive – post-obstructive diuresis may be triggered; give only if septic shock.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Unable to pass urine after catheter removal (for ≥6 h)
  - Fever, rigors, or haematuria (sepsis/blocked catheter)
  - Sudden loss of leg/ perineal sensation (cauda equina)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urology clinic in 2–6 weeks for TWOC or further workup
  - GP to review medication list and stop offending drugs

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior urology:**
  - Failed urethral catheterisation (after two attempts by trained clinician)
  - Suspected cauda equina (immediate MRI – also call neurosurgery)
  - Post-obstructive diuresis >300 mL/h for >4 h with electrolyte derangement
- **Call ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L with shock
  - GCS <13 or seizure from uraemia/hyperkalaemia
  - K+ >6.5 mmol/L (give calcium gluconate + insulin/dextrose, then catheterise)
